content.text: What Is MemGPT / Letta?

MemGPT (now shipped as the Letta framework) is an open-source agent framework designed around a tiered memory architecture. It manages three memory layers: in-context memory (always in the prompt), external recall memory (conversation history paged in on demand), and archival memory (a vector store searched via tool calls). The agent orchestrates its own memory — deciding when to page in history, search the archive, or update its core memory blocks — without the caller having to manage any of it explicitly.
Three-Tier Memory Architecture

attrs.alt: Letta three-tier memory: in-context (system prompt + recall + working), external recall store, archival vector store

content.text: Creating an Agent with Core Memory

Core memory blocks (persona and human) are injected into the system prompt on every call. They hold compact, always-visible facts about the agent's identity and the user. The LLMConfig and EmbeddingConfig connect Letta to Claude and Voyage respectively. Once created, the agent_id is a durable handle — the agent persists across sessions.

# Core memory: always-present blocks injected into the system prompt.
# The agent can read and edit these blocks via tool calls.
# Use them for persona, user profile, and session goals.

from letta import create_client, EmbeddingConfig, LLMConfig
from letta.schemas.memory import ChatMemory

client = create_client()   # connects to local Letta server

# Create an agent with two core memory blocks:
#   persona  — who the agent is
#   human    — what the agent knows about the user
agent = client.create_agent(
    name="project_assistant",
    llm_config=LLMConfig(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        model_endpoint_type="anthropic",
        model_endpoint="https://api.anthropic.com/v1",
        context_window=200_000,
    ),
    embedding_config=EmbeddingConfig(
        embedding_model="voyage-3",
        embedding_endpoint_type="voyageai",
        embedding_dim=1024,
    ),
    memory=ChatMemory(
        human="The user is a software engineer working on a Python backend project.",
        persona=(
            "I am a project assistant that helps track decisions, deadlines, "
            "and technical constraints across long-running engineering projects."
        ),
    ),
)
print(f"Agent id: {agent.id}")
Sending Messages

The caller sends plain text messages; Letta's agent loop handles the rest. Internally, the agent may call archival_memory_search() to retrieve relevant history, core_memory_replace() to update what it knows about the user, or summarize_conversation() to compress the recall buffer. All of this is transparent to the caller — the response includes the inner step messages for inspection.

# Sending messages: the agent manages its own memory transparently.
# It decides when to search archival memory, page in recall history,
# or update core memory blocks — the caller just sends messages.

from letta import create_client

client = create_client()

def chat(agent_id: str, user_message: str) -> str:
    """Send a message and return the assistant reply."""
    response = client.send_message(
        agent_id=agent_id,
        role="user",
        message=user_message,
    )
    # response.messages contains all inner steps (tool calls, etc.)
    # The final assistant reply is the last message with role="assistant"
    for msg in reversed(response.messages):
        if hasattr(msg, "text") and msg.text:
            return msg.text
    return ""

# Example conversation
agent_id = "agent-abc123"
reply1 = chat(agent_id, "The project budget is $80,000 and the deadline is December 15.")
# Agent automatically stores this in archival memory

reply2 = chat(agent_id, "What was the project budget?")
# Agent automatically searches archival memory and returns the stored fact
print(reply2)
Archival Memory: Long-Term Storage

Archival memory is an unbounded vector store. The agent inserts facts with archival_memory_insert() and retrieves them with archival_memory_search() — both are tool calls the agent makes autonomously. You can also read and write archival memory directly via the SDK for bootstrapping, testing, or administrative tasks.

# Archival memory: the agent's long-term vector store.
# The agent uses tool calls (archival_memory_search, archival_memory_insert)
# to manage it autonomously. You can also read/write it directly via the SDK.

from letta import create_client

client = create_client()
agent_id = "agent-abc123"

# Insert a fact directly (bypassing the agent's tool loop)
client.insert_archival_memory(
    agent_id=agent_id,
    memory="Sprint 4 retro: the CI pipeline failure was caused by a missing env var in the staging config.",
)

# Search archival memory directly (for inspection or testing)
results = client.get_archival_memory(
    agent_id=agent_id,
    query="CI pipeline failure",
    limit=5,
)
for r in results:
    print(f"[{r.id}] {r.text}")

# The agent uses the same data via its own archival_memory_search() tool call:
# When the user asks about CI failures, the agent calls the tool internally,
# retrieves the fact, and cites it in its response.
Reading and Updating Core Memory

Core memory blocks can be read and updated from outside the agent via the SDK. This is useful for onboarding (pre-populating user context before the first conversation) or for syncing the agent's knowledge from an external profile store. The agent also updates its own blocks mid-conversation via the core_memory_replace() and core_memory_append() tool calls.

# Core memory blocks can be read and updated at any time.
# The agent also updates them autonomously when it decides to.

from letta import create_client

client = create_client()
agent_id = "agent-abc123"

# Read current core memory
memory = client.get_in_context_memory(agent_id)
print("Human block:", memory.get_block("human").value)
print("Persona block:", memory.get_block("persona").value)

# Update a block directly (e.g., after onboarding)
client.update_in_context_memory(
    agent_id=agent_id,
    section="human",
    value=(
        "The user is a senior backend engineer, prefers Python, "
        "is working on a FastAPI + PostgreSQL project, deadline Dec 15."
    ),
)

# The agent can also update its own core memory during a conversation
# by calling the core_memory_replace() or core_memory_append() tools.
# Example: if the user says "I switched to SQLite", the agent calls
# core_memory_replace() to update the human block automatically.
Common Pitfalls
Treating Letta as a black box without inspecting inner steps

: Letta agents make tool calls internally (archival_memory_search, core_memory_replace, etc.) that are invisible unless you inspect response.messages. Log inner steps in development so you understand how the agent manages memory and can tune prompts accordingly.
Core memory block overflow

: Each core memory block has a character limit (~2000 chars by default). If the agent tries to append too much, the tool call fails silently. Keep persona and human blocks focused — detailed knowledge belongs in archival memory, not core blocks.
Confusing recall vs archival memory

: Recall memory (conversation history) is paged automatically by Letta's internal summarization. Archival memory is explicitly searched via tool calls. Don't try to store task-specific context in core memory; let the agent use archival_memory_insert for persistent facts.
Running Letta server in the same process as the application

: The Letta server manages state and background jobs. Run it as a separate process (`letta server`) or use the in-memory client only for testing. Embedding it in a production web server introduces shared state and lifecycle issues.
Multi-Agent Setup with Letta

Separate concerns into multiple Letta agents — each with its own archival memory but bootstrapped with shared project context. Route incoming messages by keyword to the appropriate specialist agent. This pattern keeps each agent's archival store focused and avoids cross-contamination between decision tracking, action item tracking, and general Q&A.

# Multi-agent setup: separate agents for different responsibilities,
# each with its own archival memory but able to share a read-only knowledge base.

from letta import create_client
from letta.schemas.memory import ChatMemory

client = create_client()

# Agent 1: tracks project decisions
decision_agent = client.create_agent(
    name='decision_tracker',
    memory=ChatMemory(
        persona='I track and recall engineering decisions made during the project.',
        human='Senior engineer, backend team.',
    ),
)

# Agent 2: tracks action items
action_agent = client.create_agent(
    name='action_tracker',
    memory=ChatMemory(
        persona='I track open action items and their owners.',
        human='Senior engineer, backend team.',
    ),
)

# Bootstrap both agents with shared project context
shared_context = (
    'Project: FastAPI backend. Budget: $80k. Deadline: Dec 15. '
    'Tech stack: Python 3.12, PostgreSQL 16, Docker.'
)
client.insert_archival_memory(decision_agent.id, shared_context)
client.insert_archival_memory(action_agent.id, shared_context)

# Route messages based on content
def route_message(message: str) -> str:
    if any(kw in message.lower() for kw in ['decided', 'agreed', 'approved']):
        return client.send_message(decision_agent.id, 'user', message).messages[-1].text
    if any(kw in message.lower() for kw in ['action', 'todo', 'assign', 'by when']):
        return client.send_message(action_agent.id,   'user', message).messages[-1].text
    # Default: use decision agent for general queries
    return client.send_message(decision_agent.id, 'user', message).messages[-1].text
Inspecting Recall and Inner Tool Calls

During development, inspect the agent's recall buffer and the inner tool calls from each response. This reveals whether the agent is calling archival_memory_search correctly, which memories it retrieved, and how much of its context window it is consuming. Use context_window_size_current vs context_window_size_max to detect agents approaching their limit before they start evicting recall history.

# Inspecting recall (conversation history) and inner tool calls
# Useful for debugging why the agent retrieved (or missed) a memory.

from letta import create_client

client = create_client()
agent_id = 'agent-abc123'

# List the last N messages in the agent's recall buffer
messages = client.get_messages(agent_id, limit=20)
for m in messages:
    print(f'[{m.role}] {str(m.text or m.tool_call or "")[:120]}')

# Inspect the last response's inner steps (tool calls)
response = client.send_message(agent_id, 'user', 'What was the budget?')
for msg in response.messages:
    if hasattr(msg, 'tool_call') and msg.tool_call:
        tc = msg.tool_call
        print(f'Tool call: {tc.name}({tc.arguments})')
    elif hasattr(msg, 'tool_return') and msg.tool_return:
        print(f'Tool return: {str(msg.tool_return)[:120]}')
    elif hasattr(msg, 'text') and msg.text and msg.role == 'assistant':
        print(f'Final reply: {msg.text}')

# Check how much context the agent is using
context_window = client.get_agent_context_window(agent_id)
print(f'Tokens used: {context_window.context_window_size_current}')
print(f'Context limit: {context_window.context_window_size_max}')
Summary
Letta (MemGPT) implements a three-tier memory model: in-context core blocks, external recall (conversation history), and archival (vector store).
The agent manages its own memory autonomously via tool calls — archival_memory_search, archival_memory_insert, core_memory_replace — without caller involvement.
Core memory blocks (persona, human) are injected into every system prompt and hold compact, always-visible context; detailed facts belong in archival memory.
Inspect response.messages in development to see inner memory management steps; use the SDK to pre-populate archival memory and update core blocks for onboarding.
Run the Letta server as a separate process from your application to avoid shared-state and lifecycle issues in production.
